notes.note: Ligarius's case became one of Cicero's most celebrated forensic efforts: the Pro Ligario, delivered before Caesar in 46 BC, was a remarkable speech addressed to a single judge who was also the prosecution's patron and the letter-writer here is the advocate preparing the ground.

notes.anchor: the fifth day before the kalends of the first intercalary month
notes.note: The date corresponds to late October 46 BC, in the extra month Caesar's reformed calendar inserted; Cicero records it with precision because the audience with Caesar was a significant event worth fixing in the record.

notes.anchor: your brothers and kinsmen lay prostrate at his feet
notes.note: Prostration (supplicatio) before a powerful patron in a private petition was an accepted Roman gesture of pleading; that Caesar's reaction was 'gentle and generous' (mollis et liberalis) was the positive signal Cicero was reporting back to Ligarius.

notes.anchor: not only Caesar but all his friends besides
notes.note: Cicero's strategy was to work Caesar's inner circle — men like Balbus and Oppius — as auxiliary channels of persuasion, a network he deployed systematically for Pompeian exiles throughout 46 BC.
